text:
Cyber-physical systems (CPS) underpin nearly all modern infrastructure, yet are more than frequently compromised in terms of their operational safety due to faults and anomalies. In this work, the suitability of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) such as Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Gemma 2 for anomaly detection in two applications — battery management and powertrain systems — is investigated.

Our approach leverages a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) paradigm with an innovative two-step method: 1) the models extract operational rules from the normal behavior data, and 2) they apply the extracted rules to uncover system faults. Experiments showed that, while using typical prompts worked well for the battery dataset, fine-tuning the prompts was necessary for the powertrain dataset to achieve the desired performance. By fine-tuning the prompts to explicitly ask for the rule extraction, we achieved higher detection accuracy. The experimental results showed that Mistral 7B achieved an F1-score of 0.99, while the Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 2 models achieved a perfect F1-score of 1.0 in several cases, which demonstrated the importance of prompt engineering and rule extraction for leveraging LLMs for fault detection and achieving the desired operational resilience for cyber-physical systems.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
